agent1_snippets.content: Mitral valve repair requires cardiopulmonary bypass and, in the vast majority of cases, full cardiac arrest. agent1_snippets.title: Transesophageal Echocardiogram -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: Transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) uses ultrasound waves to examine the heart and great vessels. There are four basic TEE rescue views that are all easy to obtain and helpful in determining the potential cause of cardiac arrest. TEE can provide valuable information quickly and at low risk, and therefore is a useful tool to improve outcomes and patient survival. This activity reviews the indications, contraindications, and technique involved in performing transesophageal echocardiography and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in the care of patients undergoing this procedure.

agent1_snippets.title: Death Certification -- Other Issues -- Brain Death
agent1_snippets.content: Brain death is defined as the irreversible cessation of all functions of the brain activities, including the brain stem. Traditionally, the concept of death has always been historically linked to the cessation of heartbeat, but with advances in technology, including mechanical ventilation and inotropic support therapy, an individual might have a beating heart but an irreversible loss of brain functions. [11] [12] Brain death declaration is a wholly clinical diagnosis. Practitioners are not required to perform another test if 2 tests for brain stem function and an apnea test are conclusive for brain death.

agent1_snippets.title: Organ Procurement From Patients With Trauma -- Clinical Significance -- Donation After Brain Death
agent1_snippets.content: The apnea test evaluates ventilatory drive. The patient is disconnected from the ventilator for 10 minutes, and a positive test is indicated by absent respiratory effort and increasing PaCO 2 levels. The apnea test should be halted, and the patient should be reconnected to the ventilator if they experience hypotension, hypoxemia, or cardiac arrhythmias. A false-positive apnea test can occur in sedated patients or those with high cervical injuries affecting diaphragmatic function. While brain death is primarily a clinical diagnosis, additional testing can support the diagnosis, especially in the presence of confounding factors. Electroencephalography (EEG) is a commonly used noninvasive method to assess brain electrical activity, and it is often conducted at the bedside. However, EEG can be significantly influenced by sedation, hypothermia, and metabolic derangements, which can limit its reliability. In contrast, cerebral angiography, transcranial Doppler ultrasound, and cerebral scintigraphy assess cerebral blood flow and are not influenced by central nervous system depression, hypothermia, or metabolic disorders. Therefore, these tests are highly useful as confirmatory tests for brain death. [40] [41]

agent1_snippets.title: Transesophageal Echocardiogram -- Technique or Treatment -- Midesophageal Bicaval View (MEBC)
agent1_snippets.content: From the MELA position, rotate the scope towards the patient’s right (clockwise if one looks from head caudally along a supine patient). This brings the left and right atria into view. This view is called the Midesophageal Bicaval (MEBC) It may require decreasing the angle of the multiplane by some 5 to 15 degrees. One will see the left atrium (LA) at the top of the screen, the right atrium (RA) in the middle of the screen, the inferior and superior vena cava, the inter-atrial septum and RA appendage. This is a good screening view to search for a clot in the IVC or RA if PE is suspected. RA size can also be assessed. The first two views, (MEFC view and the TGLV short axis view) are the most important in the setting of cardiac arrest. The EP should concentrate on mastery of these two views.

agent1_snippets.title: Echocardiogram -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Transesophageal Echocardiography (TEE) is used because it provides a superior imaging quality of the posterior heart chambers and structures when compared to TTE, can be used during coronary artery bypass graft surgery, aids in placement of transcatheter devices, and may be performed at the bedside in clinically ill patients. [11] This includes assessing valvular structures when the diagnosis of Infective endocarditis is suspected, or pre-operative evaluations of thrombus prior to cardioversion. TTE is a good initiate test of choice to evaluate cardiac structures for vegetation seen in infective endocarditis; however, if clinical suspicion is high, a TEE must be obtained to properly evaluate the valves and associated valvular structures.

agent1_snippets.title: Transesophageal Echocardiogram -- Technique or Treatment -- Midesophageal Four-Chamber View (MEFC)
agent1_snippets.content: The most diagnostically valuable, and the easiest to obtain view is the Midesophageal Four-Chamber view (MEFC). This view is obtained when the TEE probe is first inserted with the multiplane set at zero degrees, that is, not rotated. With minimal to no manipulation, one can see all four chambers of the heart, the mitral and tricuspid valves, the septal and lateral walls of the left ventricle (LV), the free wall of the right ventricle (RV), and the pericardium. This view can show the effectiveness of the chest compressions, the degree of forwarding blood flow, fluid status, ventricular function, presence/absence of fine ventricular fibrillation (Vfib), and the presence/absence of a significant pericardial effusion and possible tamponade. An extremely dilated RV with free wall akinesis and normal apical contractions (McConnell Sign) is highly diagnostic for an acute, massive PE. Sometimes a thrombus can be visualized in the RA or RV in this view. If so, perhaps a PE caused the cardiac arrest. Transesophageal echocardiogram (TEE) is not the preferred imaging study for diagnosing PE, but it does offer useful information when the patient is too unstable to go to CT or MRI. [8] [9]

agent1_snippets.title: Right Heart Failure -- Evaluation -- Echocardiography
agent1_snippets.content: Right ventricular free wall strain: This tool is also useful in assessing right ventricular function. Strain is a composite measurement of right ventricular loading and dysfunction. Abnormal strain patterns have been associated with disease progression, higher diuretic doses, and mortality in PAH. Right ventricular strain has also been shown to predict RVF after implantation of a left ventricular assist device (LVAD) and clinical outcomes in patients referred for heart transplantation.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2291
agent2_snippets.content: HEART: GENERAL AND MISCELLANEOUS OPERATIVE PROCEDURES is a broad surgical category that encompasses several specific cardiac interventions. Among its subprocedures are Temporary cardioassist with artificial heart, which involves the use of a mechanical device to provide short‑term circulatory support when the native heart cannot maintain adequate output, and Operation on heart and pericardium, which includes surgical techniques directed at repairing or modifying the myocardium, valves, coronary vessels, or the surrounding pericardial sac. Together, these child procedures illustrate the range of operative options available for managing acute cardiac failure, structural heart disease, and pericardial pathology within the overarching framework of general and miscellaneous heart surgery.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 4 -- Community 2865
agent2_snippets.content: Heart valve surgeries (عمليات صمام القلب) comprise a range of specific operative interventions. Replacement of the tricuspid valve performed with cardiopulmonary bypass (Replacement, tricuspid valve, with cardiopulmonary bypass) is a narrowly defined procedure within this category. Similarly, tricuspid valve replacement (إعاضة الصمام الثلاثي الشرف) and pulmonary valve replacement (إعاضة الصمام الرئوي) are distinct, more specific types of heart valve surgery. Cardiac valve stenosis (رأب الصمام القلبي) represents a pathological condition of the heart valves that often necessitates inclusion in the broader context of heart valve surgeries for diagnostic evaluation and potential surgical management.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 1072
agent2_snippets.content: Cardiac valvotomy is classified as a type of عملية صمام القلب (heart valve operation) and is specifically linked to استئصال تأموري (valve excision). Within the broader category of عمليات صمام القلب, several more narrowly defined procedures are recognized, including إعاضة الصمام الثلاثي الشرف (tricuspid valve replacement), إعاضة الصمام الرئوي (pulmonary valve replacement), استبدال الصمام الأبهري (aortic valve replacement), and استبدال الصمام التاجي (mitral valve replacement). These relationships illustrate that cardiac valvotomy and valve excision are part of the general spectrum of heart valve surgeries, while the named replacement procedures represent distinct, focused interventions targeting individual cardiac valves. Understanding how these procedures are organized under the umbrella of heart valve operations aids clinicians in selecting appropriate surgical strategies and communicating procedural options to patients.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 133
agent2_snippets.content: The knowledge base organizes cardiac and pericardial interventions into a hierarchical taxonomy that distinguishes broad “other or heart procedures” from more specific operative and diagnostic categories. Within the operative branch, “HEART: GENERAL AND MISCELLANEOUS OPERATIVE PROCEDURES” (a child of “other or heart procedures”) contains “Operation on heart and pericardium,” “جراحة القلب المفتوح” (open‑heart surgery), and the valve‑focused group “عمليات صمام القلب” (heart‑valve operations). “عمليات صمام القلب” includes the generic “heart valve procedures” and specific surgical actions such as Cardiac valvotomy, Excision of heart valve, valve repair (رأب الصمام القلبي), aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري), coronary valve replacement (استبدال الصمام التاجي), tricuspid valve replacement (إعاضة الصمام الثلاثي الشرف), pulmonary valve replacement (إعاضة الصمام الرئوي), and the transcatheter technique زراعة الصمام الأبهري عبر القسطرة (transcatheter aortic valve implantation).

agent2_snippets.content: Left ventricular operation represents a broad category of cardiac surgical interventions directed at the left ventricle. Within this category, the operation on mitral papillary muscle is a specific type of left ventricular operation and is further placed under the overarching therapeutic domain of valve treatment (علاج الصمام التاجي). Another distinct procedure classified as a child of left ventricular operation is the transluminal left ventricular biopsy, which involves percutaneous sampling of ventricular tissue. These hierarchical relationships illustrate how specific mitral papillary muscle interventions and transluminal biopsy techniques are organized under the general concept of left ventricular operation and linked to the broader context of valve therapy.
